title: Rabbit colony system versus cage system
subtitle: The management and welfare trade-offs between the two main housing approaches.
excerpt: Colony systems let rabbits express natural social behaviour and are easier to manage at small scale. Cage systems allow individual monitoring, reduce disease transmission, and suit intensive production. Neither is universally better; the right choice depends on the scale, the keeper's time, and the production aim.
sourceNotes: Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund, housing guidance. Cornell Small Farms, Rabbit Production for Small Farms bulletin. British Rabbit Council housing standards.

glossaryTerms.definition: A housing approach in which multiple rabbits are kept together in a large shared enclosure, typically on a solid floor with deep straw, allowing social interaction and some natural behaviour. Does must be separated to kindle.

Individual or small-group housing in wire-floor cages, allowing individual monitoring of feed intake and health. Standard in commercial rabbit production; requires individual management of breeding, lactation, and weaning.

content.text: for meat rabbits depends primarily on the number of does in production and the level of individual control the keeper wants over breeding and health.

A colony allows rabbits to express social and exploratory behaviour that a cage system does not. Grow-out kits (post-weaning rabbits kept until slaughter weight) do better in colonies than in cages: they eat more, grow faster, and show fewer stereotypic behaviours. Management is simpler: fill the colony, clean it weekly, check animals daily, remove at slaughter weight. No individual cage management is required.

Disease spreads faster in a colony. A rabbit with snuffles or coccidiosis will infect colony-mates before the illness is noticed. Does must be separated to individual quarters before kindling; mixing a doe with kits in an open colony results in other does stealing and sometimes killing kits. Breeding management in a colony requires moving does in and out, which is more labour than managing cages individually.

Individual cages allow exact monitoring of feed intake per animal, earlier detection of health problems, and precise control of breeding dates and litter sizes. Disease is contained to one animal when caught early. Cage systems are standard for commercial production because the individual monitoring they allow is worth the welfare and labour cost at scale.

For most small-scale producers, a hybrid works best: breeding does in individual cages for breeding, kindling, and lactation, then moving grow-out kits into a colony pen from weaning to slaughter weight. This captures the management control of cages for the critical breeding period while allowing the growth-rate and welfare benefits of colony housing during the grow-out.
